name: Judah ben Joseph ibn Balam
bio: Judah ben Joseph ibn Balam (c. 1000–1070) was an Andalusian Jewish grammarian, biblical commentator, poet, and philosopher active during the golden age of Sephardic Jewish scholarship in Muslim Spain. Born in Toledo and later active in Córdoba and Seville, he became one of the leading Hebrew philologists of his generation. Influenced by Arabic linguistic science and earlier Jewish grammarians such as Judah Hayyuj, he composed important works on Hebrew grammar and biblical interpretation that helped shape the development of medieval Jewish philology.

name: Isaac Alfasi
bio: Isaac ben Jacob Alfasi (1013–1103), commonly known as the Rif, was one of the greatest medieval halakhic authorities and a foundational figure in the development of Jewish law. Born near Fez in North Africa, he studied in the leading academies of Kairouan before establishing himself as a major rabbinic authority in Morocco. Late in life he relocated to al-Andalus, where he headed the yeshiva of Lucena and became one of the most influential legal scholars of Sephardic Jewry. His halakhic digest of the Talmud distilled practical law from the broader Talmudic discussions and became a cornerstone for later authorities including Maimonides and Jacob ben Asher.

name: Bahya ibn Paquda
bio: Bahya ibn Paquda was an eleventh-century Jewish philosopher, ethicist, and dayan in Muslim Spain, best known for composing one of the foundational works of Jewish ethical literature. Active in Zaragoza during the flourishing of Sephardic Jewish intellectual culture under Islamic rule, he sought to integrate rabbinic Judaism with philosophical introspection and spiritual devotion. His writings emphasized inward religious life, sincerity of worship, trust in God, and moral self-discipline, helping shape the later tradition of Jewish musar literature.

name: Judah Halevi
bio: Judah Halevi (c. 1075–1141) was one of the greatest Hebrew poets, philosophers, physicians, and religious thinkers of medieval Sephardic Judaism. Born in Tudela in Muslim Spain during the golden age of Hebrew poetry and philosophy, he became renowned for his liturgical verse, secular poetry, and deep spiritual reflections on Jewish identity and exile. Though trained in Arabic philosophy and medicine, he ultimately emphasized revelation, religious experience, and the unique spiritual destiny of the Jewish people. His philosophical dialogue 'The Kuzari' became one of the foundational works of Jewish thought, while his poetry profoundly shaped Hebrew literary tradition.

name: Joseph ibn Migash
bio: Joseph ibn Migash (1077–1141), also known as Ri Migash, was one of the foremost Talmudists of medieval Sephardic Jewry and a central transmitter of the Andalusian rabbinic tradition. Born in Seville during the height of Jewish intellectual life in Muslim Spain, he became the leading disciple of Isaac Alfasi in Lucena. Upon the death of his teacher, he succeeded him as head of the Lucena yeshiva and became renowned for his profound legal reasoning and Talmudic analysis. His teachings strongly influenced later Sephardic scholarship, including Maimonides, whose family revered him as one of the greatest sages of the era.

name: Meir ben Samuel
bio: Meir ben Samuel (c. 1085–1175) was a French Tosafist, rabbi, and scholar of the early Ashkenazic rabbinic tradition. Active in Ramerupt in northern France, he became closely associated with the school of Rashi through his marriage to Rashi's daughter Jochebed. He was the father of several leading Tosafists, including Rabbenu Tam and Rashbam, and helped shape the intellectual environment that gave rise to the Tosafist movement. His teachings and legal traditions were transmitted through his sons and students and became foundational to the development of medieval Ashkenazic Talmudic scholarship.

name: Samuel ben Meir
bio: Samuel ben Meir (c. 1085–c. 1158), commonly known as Rashbam, was one of the leading Tosafists and biblical commentators of medieval Ashkenazic Judaism. Born in Ramerupt in northern France, he was a grandson of Rashi and part of the distinguished scholarly family that shaped early Ashkenazic rabbinic culture. Rashbam became renowned for his commitment to the plain meaning of the biblical text (peshat), often offering interpretations that differed from traditional midrashic readings. Alongside his biblical commentary, he contributed significantly to Tosafist Talmudic scholarship and helped establish the intellectual foundations of northern French Judaism.

name: Abraham ibn Ezra
bio: Abraham ibn Ezra (c. 1089–1167) was one of the greatest Jewish biblical commentators, poets, grammarians, philosophers, and astronomers of medieval Spain. Born in Tudela in al-Andalus, he belonged to the flourishing intellectual culture of Sephardic Jewry during the Islamic Golden Age. A master of Hebrew grammar and philology, he composed influential biblical commentaries distinguished by their linguistic precision, rationalism, and sensitivity to literary structure. Following political upheavals and personal hardship in Muslim Spain, he spent decades traveling across North Africa, Italy, France, and England, disseminating Andalusian Jewish scholarship throughout Europe.

name: Eliezer ben Nathan
bio: Eliezer ben Nathan (c. 1090–1170), commonly known by the acronym Ra'avan, was one of the leading Talmudists and halakhic authorities of medieval Ashkenaz. Born in Mainz during the formative period of Rhineland Jewish scholarship, he studied under the disciples of Rashi and became a central figure in the transmission of early Tosafist traditions. He lived through the aftermath of the First Crusade and documented anti-Jewish persecutions in his liturgical and historical writings. His halakhic works and responsa became influential sources for later Ashkenazic rabbinic tradition.

name: Abraham ibn Daud
bio: Abraham ibn Daud (c. 1110–1180), also known as Rabad I, was a Jewish philosopher, historian, astronomer, and chronicler of medieval Spain. Born in Córdoba during the final flourishing of Jewish intellectual life under Muslim rule, he became one of the earliest Jewish Aristotelian philosophers and sought to reconcile Greek philosophy with rabbinic Judaism. Following the Almohad invasions of al-Andalus, he relocated to Christian Spain, where he continued his scholarly and communal activities. His philosophical work 'Ha-Emunah ha-Ramah' defended Judaism against Karaite and skeptical critiques while integrating Aristotelian metaphysics into Jewish theology.

name: Abraham ben David of Posquières
bio: Abraham ben David of Posquières (c. 1125–1198), commonly known by the acronym Ravad or RABaD III, was one of the foremost Provençal Talmudists and halakhic authorities of the High Middle Ages. Born in Narbonne in southern France, he studied under leading scholars of Provence before establishing a major academy in Posquières. Renowned for his penetrating Talmudic analysis and independence of thought, he became especially famous for his critical glosses on Maimonides' Mishneh Torah, defending traditional rabbinic approaches against excessive systematization. Alongside his halakhic scholarship, he also engaged deeply with Jewish mysticism and early Kabbalistic traditions.

name: Hillel ben Eliakim
bio: Hillel ben Eliakim was a medieval Jewish rabbi and biblical commentator active in the Byzantine world during the twelfth and thirteenth centuries. Likely born in Greece, he became known for his Hebrew commentaries on the Torah and other biblical books, synthesizing earlier rabbinic interpretation with linguistic and exegetical analysis. His writings reflect the transmission of Sephardic and eastern Mediterranean scholarly traditions into Byzantine Jewish communities and preserve important strands of medieval biblical interpretation.

name: Samuel ibn Tibbon
bio: Samuel ibn Tibbon (c. 1150–1230) was a Provençal Jewish philosopher, physician, and translator who played a central role in transmitting Arabic Jewish philosophy into the Hebrew-speaking world. Born in Lunel in southern France into the distinguished Ibn Tibbon family, he became renowned for his Hebrew translations of major philosophical and scientific works, especially those of Maimonides and Aristotle. His translations and commentaries profoundly shaped medieval Jewish intellectual culture in Europe and helped establish philosophical Hebrew as a literary and scholarly language.

name: David Kimhi
bio: David Kimhi (c. 1160–1235), commonly known by the acronym Radak, was one of the greatest medieval Jewish biblical commentators, grammarians, and philosophers of Provence. Born in Narbonne into a distinguished scholarly family, he was educated by his father Joseph Kimhi and later by his brother Moses Kimhi. He became renowned for his lucid Hebrew grammar, rationalist biblical exegesis, and efforts to synthesize philology with traditional rabbinic interpretation. His commentaries became foundational texts for later Jewish and Christian biblical scholarship alike.

name: Eleazar of Worms
bio: Eleazar ben Judah of Worms (c. 1165–1238), commonly known as Eleazar of Worms or the Rokeach, was one of the leading figures of the medieval Ashkenazic pietist movement known as the Hasidei Ashkenaz. Born in Mainz into a distinguished rabbinic family, he studied under Judah ben Samuel of Regensburg and became a major authority in Talmudic scholarship, Jewish ethics, liturgy, and mysticism. Deeply marked by the violence of the Crusading era, including the murder of members of his family, he developed an intense spiritual and ascetic worldview that profoundly influenced later Jewish mystical traditions.

name: Yonah Gerondi
bio: Yonah Gerondi (c. 1200–1263), also known as Rabbeinu Yonah, was one of the foremost medieval rabbis, ethicists, and Talmudists of Sephardic Judaism. Born in Girona in Catalonia, he studied under leading French Tosafists and became deeply involved in the rabbinic academies of Spain and Provence. Initially associated with opposition to the philosophical works of Maimonides during the Maimonidean controversies, he later repented after witnessing the burning of Talmud manuscripts in Paris and came to regard the persecutions as divine punishment for attacking Maimonides. His ethical and halakhic writings became foundational texts of Jewish moral and spiritual literature.

name: Aaron HaLevi of Barcelona
bio: Aaron HaLevi of Barcelona (c. 1235–c. 1290), commonly known as the Ra'ah, was a prominent Catalonian rabbi, Talmudist, and halakhic authority of medieval Sephardic Judaism. Born in Barcelona during the flourishing of Jewish intellectual life in Christian Spain, he studied under Jonah Gerondi and Nahmanides and became one of the leading rabbinic scholars of Catalonia. He was known for his sharp Talmudic analysis and independent legal reasoning, often engaging critically with the interpretations of Solomon ben Adret. His teachings and writings contributed significantly to the development of medieval Sephardic halakhic scholarship.

name: Menachem Meiri
bio: Menachem Meiri (1249–1316), commonly known as the Meiri, was one of the foremost Provençal Talmudists, halakhists, and philosophers of the medieval Jewish world. Born in Perpignan in southern France, he studied under leading Provençal scholars and became renowned for his lucid and systematic approach to Talmudic interpretation. Deeply influenced by both rabbinic tradition and rationalist philosophy, he sought to harmonize legal scholarship with ethical universalism and intellectual clarity. His extensive commentaries on the Talmud became notable for their organization, accessibility, and comparatively tolerant treatment of non-Jewish societies.

name: Abba Mari ben Moses ben Joseph
bio: Abba Mari ben Moses ben Joseph (c. 1250–c. 1306), also known as Abba Mari ha-Yarhi or Don Astruc of Lunel, was a Provençal rabbi, theologian, and polemicist active during the Maimonidean controversies in southern France and Spain. Born in Lunel in Provence, he descended from a distinguished rabbinic family and became deeply learned in Talmudic literature and Jewish philosophy. Though familiar with the rationalist tradition of Maimonides, he increasingly aligned with the more traditionalist and mystical orientation associated with Nachmanides and became alarmed by the spread of radical Aristotelianism among Jewish intellectuals in Provence.

name: Mordecai ben Hillel
bio: Mordecai ben Hillel (c. 1250–1298), commonly known simply as the Mordecai, was a leading German Talmudist and halakhic authority of medieval Ashkenazic Judaism. Born in the Holy Roman Empire, he studied under major Tosafist scholars including Meir of Rothenburg and became one of the foremost transmitters of late Tosafist legal traditions. His comprehensive halakhic compilation on the Talmud synthesized rulings and customs from German and French rabbinic authorities and became an important source for later codifiers. He and much of his family were killed during the Rintfleisch massacres that devastated Jewish communities in Germany at the end of the thirteenth century.

name: Yom Tov of Seville
bio: Yom Tov ben Abraham Ishbili (c. 1250–1330), commonly known as the Ritva, was one of the foremost Talmudists and halakhic authorities of medieval Sephardic Judaism. Born in Seville in Christian Spain, he later became active in Barcelona and other centers of Catalonian Jewish scholarship. A disciple of Solomon ben Aderet and Aaron HaLevi, he combined rigorous Talmudic analysis with broad familiarity with earlier Sephardic and Ashkenazic traditions. His Talmudic novellae became among the most influential commentaries in the Sephardic rabbinic tradition and remain central to advanced Talmud study.

name: Asher ben Jehiel
bio: Asher ben Jehiel (c. 1250–1327), commonly known as the Rosh, was one of the foremost medieval rabbinic authorities of Ashkenazic and Sephardic Judaism. Born in the Holy Roman Empire, likely in Cologne, he studied under Rabbi Meir of Rothenburg and became a leading Talmudic scholar in Germany. Following the imprisonment of his teacher and increasing persecutions against Jews in the German lands, he fled to Christian Spain, where he became rabbi of Toledo and established a major yeshiva. His halakhic rulings and Talmudic commentaries profoundly influenced later Jewish law, particularly through their incorporation into the Arba'ah Turim and later the Shulchan Arukh.

name: Jacob ben Asher
bio: Jacob ben Asher (c. 1269–1343), commonly known as the בעל הטורים (Ba'al HaTurim), was one of the most influential medieval halakhic codifiers of Jewish law. Born in Cologne in the Holy Roman Empire, he was the son of the eminent rabbinic authority Asher ben Jehiel. Following the persecutions and instability affecting German Jewry, he migrated with his father to Toledo in Spain, where he became immersed in the Sephardic intellectual world while preserving Ashkenazic legal traditions. His monumental legal code, the 'Arba'ah Turim', systematized Jewish law into four sections and became one of the principal foundations for later halakhic works, including the Shulchan Arukh.

name: Gersonides
bio: Levi ben Gershon (1288–1344), commonly known as Gersonides or by the acronym Ralbag, was a medieval Jewish philosopher, biblical commentator, mathematician, astronomer, and physician from Provence. Born in Bagnols-sur-Cèze in southern France, he became one of the foremost Jewish Aristotelian thinkers after Maimonides. Deeply influenced by philosophy and the sciences of the Islamic and Christian worlds, he sought to harmonize reason, astronomy, and Jewish theology. His works on mathematics and astronomy gained recognition beyond Jewish circles, while his biblical commentaries became enduring classics of rationalist Jewish exegesis.

name: Nissim of Gerona
bio: Nissim ben Reuben of Gerona (c. 1310–1376), commonly known as the Ran, was one of the foremost rabbinic authorities, Talmudists, philosophers, and communal leaders of medieval Sephardic Jewry. Born in Barcelona and later active in Gerona, he studied under leading Catalonian scholars and became a central figure in the transmission of Spanish rabbinic learning during a period of political instability and growing anti-Jewish pressure. Renowned for his Talmudic novellae, halakhic rulings, and philosophical sermons, he combined rigorous legal analysis with broader ethical and theological reflection. His teachings strongly influenced later Sephardic scholarship and Jewish legal tradition.

name: Hasdai Crescas
bio: Hasdai Crescas (c. 1340–1410/1411) was a Spanish Jewish philosopher, halakhist, communal leader, and one of the most important critics of Aristotelian philosophy in medieval Judaism. Born in Barcelona during the later flourishing of Sephardic intellectual life, he studied under Nissim ben Reuben and became a prominent rabbinic authority in the Crown of Aragon. Deeply affected by the anti-Jewish persecutions of 1391, including the murder of his son, Crescas devoted himself to strengthening Jewish faith and communal resilience. His philosophical masterpiece 'Or Adonai' challenged Aristotelian metaphysics and influenced later Jewish and European thought, including Spinoza.

name: Joseph Albo
bio: Joseph Albo (c. 1380–1444) was a Spanish Jewish philosopher, theologian, and rabbi best known for his systematic presentation of the principles of Judaism in the late medieval period. Born in the Crown of Aragon, likely in Monreal del Campo, he studied under Hasdai Crescas and became deeply influenced by anti-Aristotelian Jewish philosophy. Albo lived during a period of increasing pressure on Spanish Jewry, including forced disputations and mass conversions. His major philosophical work, 'Sefer Ha-Ikkarim', sought to define the essential principles of Jewish faith while defending Judaism against Christian polemics and internal philosophical disputes.

name: Israel Isserlein
bio: Israel Isserlein (1390–1460), also known as Maharai, was one of the foremost Ashkenazic halakhic authorities of the late medieval period. Born in Regensburg during an era of upheaval for Central European Jewry, he studied under leading German and Austrian rabbis and became renowned for his mastery of Talmudic law and communal practice. He later settled in Wiener Neustadt, where he headed a major yeshiva that attracted students from across the Holy Roman Empire. His responsa and legal rulings became highly influential in shaping later Ashkenazic custom and halakhic practice, particularly through their incorporation into the works of Moses Isserles.

name: Isaac Abravanel
bio: Isaac Abravanel (1437–1508), also known as Don Isaac Abravanel, was a Portuguese Jewish statesman, financier, biblical commentator, and philosopher who played a major role in both Jewish and Iberian political history during the late medieval period. Born in Lisbon into a distinguished Sephardic family descended from Iberian Jewish nobility, he served in the royal courts of Portugal, Castile, and Naples while simultaneously producing major works of biblical exegesis and Jewish philosophy. Following the expulsion of the Jews from Spain in 1492, he became one of the leading voices of Sephardic exile and renewal in the Mediterranean world.

name: Jacob Berab
bio: Jacob Berab (1474–1546) was a leading Sephardic rabbi, Talmudist, and halakhic authority of the early Ottoman period who played a central role in the revival of Jewish scholarship in Safed after the expulsion from Spain. Born in the Kingdom of Castile shortly before the expulsion of Spanish Jewry, he fled with his family to North Africa and later studied in the Ottoman Empire. He became renowned for his Talmudic brilliance and communal leadership, serving as chief rabbi in several communities before settling in Safed. There he attempted to revive the ancient institution of semikhah (rabbinic ordination), a controversial initiative that sparked major halakhic debates throughout the Jewish world.

name: David ben Solomon ibn Abi Zimra
bio: David ben Solomon ibn Abi Zimra (c. 1479–1573), commonly known as the Radbaz, was one of the foremost halakhic authorities of the early modern Sephardic world. Born in Spain shortly before the expulsion of the Jews in 1492, he migrated with his family to North Africa and later settled in Egypt, where he became chief rabbi of Cairo and head of a major yeshiva. Renowned for his vast halakhic responsa, he addressed legal, communal, and theological questions arising across the Ottoman and Mediterranean Jewish worlds. In his later years he relocated to Safed in the Land of Israel, joining the flourishing center of rabbinic scholarship and mysticism there.

name: Amram ben Sheshna
bio: Amram ben Sheshna (c. 810–875), commonly known as Amram Gaon, was one of the leading Geonim of the Babylonian academies and served as Gaon of Sura during the ninth century. Born in Babylonia under the Abbasid Caliphate, he became renowned for his halakhic authority and liturgical scholarship. He is best remembered for compiling one of the earliest complete Jewish prayer books, known as the Seder Rav Amram, which systematized Babylonian liturgical traditions and profoundly influenced later Jewish prayer rites throughout the Diaspora.

name: Saadia Gaon
bio: Saadia ben Joseph Gaon (882–942), commonly known as Saadia Gaon, was one of the greatest Jewish philosophers, grammarians, biblical translators, and Geonic leaders of the early medieval period. Born in al-Fayyum in Egypt, he emerged as a major defender of rabbinic Judaism against Karaite criticism and played a central role in shaping medieval Jewish intellectual life. He later became Gaon of the Sura academy in Babylonia, where he combined Talmudic leadership with pioneering works in philosophy, Hebrew linguistics, biblical exegesis, and liturgy. His synthesis of rational philosophy and traditional Judaism laid the foundations for later Jewish philosophical thought.

name: Dunash ben Labrat
bio: Dunash ben Labrat (c. 920–990) was a pioneering medieval Jewish poet, grammarian, and philologist associated with the early flowering of Hebrew literature in Muslim Spain. Born in Fez in North Africa, he studied under the great grammarian Saadia Gaon in Babylonia before relocating to al-Andalus. There he became associated with the court of Hasdai ibn Shaprut in Córdoba and introduced Arabic poetic meters and linguistic methods into Hebrew poetry, helping transform medieval Hebrew literary culture. His grammatical and poetic innovations sparked enduring scholarly debates and deeply influenced later Sephardic Hebrew poetry.

name: Nissim ben Jacob
bio: Nissim ben Jacob ben Nissim ibn Shahin (990–1062), commonly known as Nissim Gaon, was one of the leading rabbinic authorities of North African Jewry during the final flourishing of the Kairouan academy. Born in Kairouan in present-day Tunisia, he studied under his father and under Chananel ben Chushiel, becoming a major transmitter of Geonic scholarship to later Sephardic traditions. Renowned for his Talmudic expertise, halakhic responsa, and ethical writings, he maintained correspondence with Jewish communities across the Mediterranean and helped preserve the intellectual legacy of the Babylonian academies.

name: Chananel ben Chushiel
bio: Chananel ben Chushiel (c. 990–1053) was a leading North African rabbi, Talmudist, and commentator of the early medieval period. Born in Kairouan in present-day Tunisia, he studied under his father Chushiel ben Elchanan and became one of the foremost scholars of the Kairouan academy, a major center of Jewish learning in the Islamic world. He is best known for composing one of the earliest systematic commentaries on the Babylonian Talmud, helping transmit Geonic traditions and methods of interpretation to later Jewish scholarship in both Sephardic and Ashkenazic communities.

name: Dayan Yechezkel Abramsky
bio: Haredi rabbi, dayan of the London Beth Din, rosh yeshiva in Jerusalem, author of Chazon Yechezkel on the Tosefta. Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yehezkel_Abramsky https://rabbiaubreyhersh.podbean.com/e/dayan-yechezkel-abramsky-part-one-1886-1931/

name: Alter of Liadi
bio: Rabbi Shneur Zalman of Liadi (1745–1812), known as the 'Alter Rebbe,' was the founder of Chabad Hasidism and author of the Tanya and Shulchan Aruch HaRav. He was a leading disciple of the Maggid of Mezeritch.

name: Arizal
bio: Rabbi Yitzchak Luria Ashkenazi (1534–1572), known as the Arizal or HaAri, was the foremost kabbalist of Safed and the founder of Lurianic Kabbalah. Though he authored little himself, his teachings as recorded by his disciple Rabbi Chaim Vital revolutionized Jewish mysticism and shaped later Hasidic and Kabbalistic thought.

name: Aruch HaShulchan
bio: Rabbi Yechiel Michel Epstein (1829–1908) was a Lithuanian rabbi and posek, best known as the author of 'Aruch HaShulchan', a monumental halakhic work covering all four sections of the Shulchan Aruch. He served as rabbi of Novogrudok for over 34 years, where he was recognized as a leading halakhic authority of his time. He was also the brother-in-law of the Netziv of Volozhin.  https://jsoundbites.podbean.com/e/the-life-legacy-of-the-aruch-hashulchan/

name: Abarbanel
bio: Don Isaac Abarbanel (1437–1508) was a Portuguese-Jewish statesman, philosopher, and biblical commentator. He served as royal treasurer in Portugal, advisor to Ferdinand and Isabella of Spain, and later settled in Italy. He was a fierce opponent of Maimonidean rationalism, emphasizing redemption and messianic hope in his works. His biblical commentaries are widely studied for their historical and philosophical insights.

name: Baal Shem Tov
bio: Rabbi Yisrael ben Eliezer (c. 1690–1700 – 1760), known as the Baal Shem Tov, was the founder of the Hasidic movement and one of the most influential Jewish leaders in Eastern Europe. Born in Podolia, he became known as a Baal Shem (folk healer using holy names and Kabbalah) and later revealed himself as a spiritual leader who emphasized joy in serving God, deep intention in prayer, and the sanctity of Torah study. From 1740 until his death he lived in Mezhibuz, attracting a circle of disciples who developed Hasidism after him, including Rabbi Yaakov Yosef of Polnoye and Rabbi Dov Ber, the Maggid of Mezritch. He passed away on Shavuot, 6 Sivan 5510 (May 21, 1760), and was buried in Mezhibuz.

name: ישראל אבוחצירא (Baba Sali)
bio: Rabbi Yisrael Abuhatzeira (1889–1984), known as the Baba Sali, was a revered Moroccan-Israeli kabbalist and tzaddik. Famous for his piety, blessings, and miracles, he became a spiritual leader for Moroccan Jewry and later for Jews throughout Israel. His yahrzeit is widely commemorated with mass pilgrimages to his tomb in Netivot.

name: יואל סירקיש (Bach)
bio: Rabbi Joel ben Samuel Sirkis (1561–1640), known as the Bach after his halakhic work Bayit Chadash, was a leading Polish rabbi and posek. He served as rabbi of several major communities including Belz, Brest-Litovsk, and Kraków. His Bayit Chadash critically analyzed the Tur, correcting errors and integrating halakhic rulings. He was also the father-in-law of the Taz.

name: Rabbi Hayyim Samuel Jacob Falk (Baal Shem of London)
bio: Rabbi Hayyim Samuel Jacob Falk (1708–1782), known as the Baal Shem of London and Doctor Falckon, was a rabbi, kabbalist, alchemist, and wonder-worker. Born in either Fürth (Bavaria) or Pidhaytsi (Podolia), he narrowly escaped execution in Westphalia on charges of sorcery and was sheltered by Count Alexander von Rantzau in Holzminden, where his mystical feats were witnessed by noblemen in 1736. Later he settled in London, living in Prescott Street and Wellclose Square, and was said to have influenced Emanuel Swedenborg. Accused by some, including Rabbi Jacob Emden, of Sabbatean associations, he nonetheless became a legendary figure in Jewish folklore. He died on 17 April 1782 and was buried in Alderney Road Cemetery, London. Falk left a bequest of £100 annually to the Great Synagogue of London and several Torah scrolls. His diary, recording kabbalistic dreams and angelic names, survives in the United Synagogue library.

name: Haham Isaac Nieto
bio: Isaac Nieto (1702–1774) was Haham of the Portuguese congregation Sha'are Hashamayim (Bevis Marks), London, and the son of David Nieto. He was officially appointed as Haham in 1733 but resigned in 1741 and travelled abroad. Returning in 1747, he became a notary and later, in 1749, established the Shaar Hashamayim congregation in Gibraltar, becoming its first rabbi. In 1751 he returned to London to serve as Av Bet Din, but resigned in 1757 after disputes. Nieto also preached a notable sermon in 1756 and published important Spanish-English translations of the prayer book. He died in London in 1774.

name: Chacham Tzvi (Rabbi Zvi Ashkenazi)
bio: Rabbi Zvi Ashkenazi (1656–1718), known as the Chacham Tzvi, was a renowned Talmudist and halakhic authority. He served as Chief Rabbi of Amsterdam and opposed the spread of Sabbateanism.

name: החלבן (Rav Yaakov Moshe Hillel)
bio: Rav Yaakov Moshe Hillel (b. 1945), known as the 'Chalban' (the Milkman), is a leading contemporary kabbalist in Jerusalem. He heads Yeshivat Ahavat Shalom, has authored numerous works on Kabbalah and Jewish thought, and is renowned for his spiritual guidance. His nickname 'Chalban' came from his family’s dairy business.

name: R' Avraham Yeshaya Karelitz
bio: Rabbi Avraham Yeshaya Karelitz (1878–1953), known as the Chazon Ish after his magnum opus, was the leading Haredi authority in Eretz Yisrael during the mid-20th century. Born in Kossow, Poland, he became renowned in Europe for his penetrating Torah works though he held no official rabbinic post. In 1933, he immigrated to Bnei Brak, where he quickly became the undisputed halachic and spiritual leader of the Yishuv’s Haredi community. He guided religious policy on issues such as army service, medicine, agriculture (including Shemitta), and yeshiva life, and laid the foundations for the modern Torah community in Israel. His home became a center of guidance for rabbis, doctors, and politicians alike.

name: Haim Yosef David Azulai (Hida)
bio: Rabbi Haim Yosef David Azulai (1724–1806), known by the acronym Hida, was a Jerusalem-born Sephardic rabbi, halakhist, kabbalist, bibliophile, and prolific author. A descendant of Rabbi Abraham Azulai, he studied under leading sages of Jerusalem including Rabbi Haim ibn Attar (Ohr HaHaim), Rabbi Shalom Sharabi, and Rabbi Yonah Nabon. Gifted with an extraordinary memory, he mastered Talmud, halakha, kabbalah, and Jewish history at a young age. Chosen as an emissary (shadar) for the Hebron community, he traveled extensively across Europe and North Africa (1753–1757; 1772–1778), strengthening ties with Diaspora communities and collecting funds for the Holy Land. His travel diaries, Ma’agal Tov, record Jewish life and his encounters with manuscripts, libraries, and rabbis across Europe. He authored over 70 works including halakhic commentaries such as Birkei Yosef, homiletics, liturgy, kabbalistic texts, and the seminal bibliographic-historical dictionary Shem HaGedolim, which preserves invaluable information on Jewish authors and texts. After his second marriage in Pisa in 1778, he settled in Livorno, a center of Jewish publishing, where he lived until his death in 1806. In 1960, his remains were reinterred in Jerusalem on Har HaMenuchot, fulfilling his wish to return to the Land of Israel.

name: Yisrael Meir ha-Kohen Kagan (Chofetz Chaim)
bio: Influential halachic authority, ethicist, and founder of the Radin Yeshiva.

name: Dayan Chanoch Ehrentreu
bio: German-born British Orthodox rabbi. He served as head (Rosh) of the London Beth Din (1984–2006), where he was a leading halachic authority, especially in medical ethics, conversions, and kashrut. He established the Sunderland Kollel, served as Av Beth Din in Manchester, and later guided the creation of the North-West London eruv.

name: Rabbi Jacob Emden
bio: Rabbi Jacob Emden (1697–1776) was a German rabbi, scholar, and fierce opponent of Sabbateanism. A prolific writer, he is remembered for his halakhic responsa, polemics, and for his heated disputes with Rabbi Jonathan Eybeschutz.

name: Rabbi Jonathan Eybeschutz
bio: Rabbi Jonathan Eybeschutz (1690–1764) was a prominent rabbi, preacher, and Talmudist, serving as Rabbi of Metz and later as Chief Rabbi of the Three Communities (Altona, Hamburg, Wandsbek). Famous for his erudition, he was embroiled in the Emden-Eybeschutz controversy.

name: Rabbi Eliyahu ben Shlomo Zalman (Vilna Gaon, Gra)
bio: Rabbi Eliyahu ben Shlomo Zalman (1720–1797), known as the Vilna Gaon or simply 'the Gra', was the preeminent Lithuanian Talmudist, halakhist, kabbalist, and leader of the Misnagdic (non-Hasidic) world. Born in Sialiec near Brisk, he showed prodigious genius from childhood, mastering Tanakh, Talmud, and Kabbalah by age ten. He lived most of his life in Vilna, where he devoted himself entirely to Torah study, refusing formal rabbinic office but guiding Lithuanian Jewry through his writings and students. His glosses and emendations to the Talmud, Shulchan Aruch, Tanakh, and kabbalistic works revolutionized Jewish study. The Vilna Gaon fiercely opposed the Hasidic movement, signing bans of excommunication in 1772 and 1781, and his disciples founded the great Lithuanian yeshiva tradition, beginning with Rabbi Chaim of Volozhin. He also valued mathematics, astronomy, and other sciences as aids to Torah but condemned secular philosophy. He passed away in Vilna on 9 October 1797 (19 Tishrei 5558). His influence continues through the 'minhag ha-Gra' and his central place in the Litvak tradition.

name: Hermann Adler HaKohen
bio: Chief Rabbi of the British Empire (1891–1911). Son and successor of Chief Rabbi Nathan Marcus Adler. A defender of modernized orthodoxy, Anglo-Jewish history, and Jewish rights. Remembered for raising the dignity and prominence of the Chief Rabbinate.

name: ר׳ אברהם מרדכי אלתר (Imrei Emes)
bio: Rav Avraham Mordechai Alter (1866–1948), known as the Imrei Emes after his Torah work, was the third Rebbe of Ger and one of the most influential Hasidic leaders of the 20th century. Under his leadership, Ger became the largest Hasidic dynasty in prewar Poland, with tens of thousands of followers. He rebuilt Ger after the Holocaust in Eretz Yisrael, continuing his dynasty's influence.

name: Immanuel Jakobovits
bio: Immanuel Jakobovits, Baron Jakobovits (1921–1999), was Chief Rabbi of the United Hebrew Congregations of the Commonwealth (1967–1991). Previously Chief Rabbi of Ireland and rabbi of the Fifth Avenue Synagogue in New York, he became the first Chief Rabbi to sit in the House of Lords. He was a pioneer in Jewish medical ethics, a conservative public voice in Britain, and close to Margaret Thatcher.

name: Rabbi Levi Yitzchok of Berditchev (Kedushas Levi)
bio: Rabbi Levi Yitzchok of Berditchev (1740–1809), also known as the Kedushas Levi and the 'Holy Berdichever,' was one of the most beloved Hasidic masters and leaders of Eastern European Jewry. A close disciple of the Maggid of Mezritch and Rabbi Shmelke of Nikolsburg, he served as rabbi in Ryczywół, Żelechów, Pinsk, and most famously Berdichev, where he became known as the 'defense attorney of the Jewish people' for his compassionate prayers and advocacy on behalf of all Jews before God. Nachman of Breslov called him the 'Glory of Israel.' He was known for fiery devotion, boundless love for every Jew, and authored the Hasidic classic 'Kedushas Levi,' a Torah commentary blending Halakhah, Midrash, and Hasidic philosophy. His songs, including 'A Dude'le' and 'A Din Toyre mit Gott,' remain part of Jewish folk and spiritual heritage.

name: Rabbi Aryeh Leib Kahana Heller (Ketzos HaChoshen)
bio: Rabbi Aryeh Leib ben Yosef Kahana Heller (c. 1745–1812) was a Galician rabbi, Talmudist, and halachist, best known for his magnum opus 'Ketzos HaChoshen.' Born in Kalush, he was a fifth-generation descendant of Rabbi Yom-Tov Lipmann Heller and the youngest of five brothers, including Rabbi Yehuda Heller, author of 'Kuntras HaSfeikos.' He studied under Rabbi Meshulam Igra of Tysmienica, becoming recognized as one of the foremost halachic authorities of his era. From 1788 until his death in 1812, he served as rabbi of Stryi, where he often engaged in halachic debates with his contemporary Rabbi Yaakov Lorberbaum, author of 'Netivot ha-Mishpat.' A staunch misnaged and critic of Hasidism, his works became classics studied in all yeshivas, laying the groundwork for analytical Talmudic methodology.

name: ר' אברהם די בוטון (Abraham de Boton, Lechem Mishneh)
bio: Rabbi Abraham de Boton (c. 1560–c. 1605) was a leading Sephardic rabbi in the Ottoman Empire and the author of Lechem Mishneh, a foundational commentary on Rambam’s Mishneh Torah. He lived and taught in Salonika and later in Adrianople (Edirne), where he headed a prominent yeshiva. His Lechem Mishneh is printed in virtually all standard editions of the Mishneh Torah and remains a central commentary in halakhic study.

name: מרדכי יפה (Mordechai Yoffe, the Levush)
bio: Rabbi Mordechai Yoffe (c.1530–1612), known as the Levush, was a major rabbinic authority and halakhist of Poland and Lithuania. A disciple of the Rema and Maharshal, he served as rabbi in Prague, Grodno, Lublin, and other communities. He is best remembered for his halakhic code 'Levushim' (Ten Garments), which systematized Jewish law after the Shulchan Aruch while incorporating Ashkenazic customs.

name: Menachem Mendel Schneerson
bio: Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneerson (1902–1994), the seventh Lubavitcher Rebbe, was one of the most influential Jewish leaders of the 20th century. Born in the Russian Empire, he studied in Berlin and Paris before escaping Nazi-occupied Europe to the United States in 1941. In 1951 he assumed leadership of Chabad-Lubavitch, transforming it into a global movement dedicated to Jewish outreach, education, and religious life. Under his leadership, Chabad established thousands of institutions worldwide, and his teachings continue to shape contemporary Judaism.

name: ר' וידאל די טולוסה (Vidal of Tolosa, Maggid Mishneh)
bio: Rabbi Vidal of Tolosa (14th century), known as the Maggid Mishneh, was a Spanish Talmudist and one of the foremost commentators on Rambam’s Mishneh Torah. His commentary, Maggid Mishneh, was widely accepted and printed in nearly all standard editions of the Mishneh Torah, becoming one of the classic works for understanding Maimonides’ rulings. He lived in Aragon (likely in Barcelona or surrounding regions) during the period of the Rishonim.

name: Rabbi Dov Ber ben Avraham of Mezeritch (Maggid of Mezeritch)
bio: Rabbi Dov Ber ben Avraham of Mezeritch (d. 1772), known as the Maggid of Mezeritch, was the leading disciple of the Baal Shem Tov and his successor as head of the early Hasidic movement. Born in Lokachi, Volhynia, around 1710, he was originally an ascetic and kabbalist before joining the Baal Shem Tov, who persuaded him to embrace a life of spiritual joy. Crippled by illness, he nonetheless became the principal architect of Hasidism, establishing his court in Mezeritch and gathering the Chevraia Kadisha, a circle of brilliant disciples who would spread Hasidism across Eastern Europe. His teachings were preserved by his students in works such as Maggid Devarav le-Yaakov, Or Torah, and Likkutim Yekarim. He died on 15 December 1772 and was buried in Hannopil beside his disciple Rabbi Zusha of Hanipol.

name: יהודה ליווא בן בצלאל (Maharal of Prague)
bio: Rabbi Judah Loew ben Bezalel (ca. 1520–1609), known as the Maharal of Prague, was a leading rabbinic authority, philosopher, kabbalist, and communal leader. He is famous for his works on ethics, philosophy, and mysticism, and in legend, for creating the Golem of Prague.

name: מאיר בן ברוך מרוטנבורג (Maharam of Rothenburg)
bio: Rabbi Meir ben Baruch (c.1215–1293), known as the Maharam of Rothenburg, was the leading German rabbi of his era, a Tosafist, and halakhic authority. He wrote responsa that shaped Ashkenazi halakhah for centuries. Imprisoned by Emperor Rudolf I, he refused ransom to prevent further persecution of Jews, and died in captivity.

name: שמואל אליעזר איידלס (Maharsha)
bio: Rabbi Shmuel Eidels (1555–1631), known as the Maharsha, was one of the greatest Talmudic commentators. His glosses on the Talmud, Chiddushei Halachot and Chiddushei Aggadot, are printed in virtually every edition of the Talmud. He served as rosh yeshivah in several Polish–Lithuanian communities, supported by his wealthy mother-in-law, Eidels, whose name he adopted.

name: שלמה לוריא (Maharshal)
bio: Rabbi Shlomo Luria (1510–1573), known as the Maharshal, was one of the greatest halakhists of Poland. He served as head of the yeshivah in Lublin, where many leading scholars studied under him. He authored Yam shel Shlomo, a major halakhic commentary on the Talmud, and Chokhmat Shlomo on the Shulchan Aruch.

name: שמואל מרגליות (Shmuel Margolies)
bio: Rabbi Shmuel ben Uri Shraga Feivish Margolies (1711–1790), known as the Maharam Margolies, was a prominent rabbi in Galicia and later one of the first great rabbinic authorities in the American colonies. He immigrated to New York in 1730s and served as rabbi of Congregation Shearith Israel, the Spanish-Portuguese synagogue, making him one of the earliest recognized rabbis in North America.

name: Meir Simcha HaKohen of Dvinsk
bio: Rabbi Meir Simcha HaKohen of Dvinsk (1843–1926) was one of the leading halachic authorities of his generation. Known as the Ohr Somayach after his magnum opus on Rambam’s Mishneh Torah, he served as Rabbi of the non-Hasidic community of Dvinsk for nearly 40 years. His second major work, Meshech Chochma, offered profound insights on the Torah and addressed contemporary challenges. He supported Religious Zionism after the Balfour Declaration, warning against over-assimilation with his famous phrase about Jews seeing 'Berlin as Jerusalem.' His works remain foundational in Torah study, and the global Ohr Somayach yeshiva network is named in his honor.

name: Chaim Elazar Spira
bio: Rabbi Chaim Elazar Spira (1868–1937) was the Rebbe of the Munkacs Hasidic dynasty and a leading rabbinic authority in interwar Europe. Author of the monumental halachic work 'Minchas Elazar', he was renowned for his sharp style, uncompromising positions, and strong opposition to Zionism and Agudath Israel. He built major Torah institutions in Munkács, including the Darchei Teshuva yeshiva and Machzike Torah schools. His leadership culminated in the famous 1933 wedding of his only daughter, attended by tens of thousands. Though he passed away in 1937, the Munkacs dynasty continues today, led by his grandson Rabbi Moshe Leib Rabinovich from Brooklyn.

name: Naftali Zvi Yehuda Berlin (Netziv)
bio: Rabbi Naftali Zvi Yehuda Berlin (1816–1893), known as the Netziv, was Rosh Yeshiva of Volozhin for 40 years, a leading Torah scholar, author of Haamek Davar, Haamek She'elah, and Meishiv Davar, and an early supporter of Chovevei Zion.

name: Rabbi David Nieto
bio: Rabbi David Nieto (1654–1728) was the Haham of the Spanish and Portuguese Jewish community of London. A polymath, he wrote works on astronomy, medicine, and theology, defending traditional Judaism against deist and Karaite challenges.

name: Rabbi Elimelech Weisblum of Lizhensk (Noam Elimelech)
bio: Rabbi Elimelech Weisblum of Lizhensk (1717–1787) was one of the great founding Rebbes of the Hasidic movement and the leading disciple of Rabbi Dov Ber of Mezeritch, the Maggid of Mezeritch. Born in Tyczyn, Poland, he became renowned for his piety, asceticism, and deep mystical scholarship. Together with his brother, Rabbi Meshulam Zushya of Anipoli, he embodied contrasting Hasidic ideals: Elimelech as the disciplined scholar and Zushya as the saintly simpleton. After the passing of his master in 1772, Elimelech established Hasidism in Poland-Galicia, training disciples such as the Chozeh of Lublin, the Kozhnitzer Maggid, and Menachem Mendel of Rimanov, who spread Hasidism throughout Poland. His Torah commentary 'Noam Elimelech' articulated the doctrine of the Tzaddik as spiritual leader and intercessor, shaping Hasidism for generations. He passed away in Leżajsk on 21 Adar 5547 (March 11, 1787). His grave is a pilgrimage site for thousands of Hasidim every year on his yahrzeit.

name: יחזקאל לנדא (Yechezkel Landau, Noda B'Yehuda)
bio: Rabbi Yechezkel Landau (1713–1793), known by the title of his responsa 'Noda B’Yehuda,' was one of the foremost halakhic authorities of the 18th century. He served as Chief Rabbi of Prague and became renowned across Europe for his rulings and leadership.

name: Rabbi Chaim ibn Attar (Ohr HaChaim)
bio: Rabbi Chaim ibn Attar (1696–1743), author of the 'Ohr HaChaim' commentary on the Torah, was a Moroccan rabbi and kabbalist who later moved to Jerusalem, where he established a famous yeshiva.

name: Kalonymus Kalman Szapiro
bio: Rabbi Kalonymus Kalman Szapiro (1889–1943), known as the Piaseczner Rebbe, was a Hasidic leader, educator, and one of the most profound spiritual voices of the Holocaust. A grandson of the Maor VaShemesh, he became rabbi of Piaseczno near Warsaw and founded the yeshiva Da’as Moshe. His works, including Chovas HaTalmidim and Bnei Machshava Tova, reshaped Hasidic education for modern youth. Confined in the Warsaw Ghetto during World War II, he sustained his followers with clandestine Torah discourses later published as 'Esh Kodesh,' a unique testimony of faith under persecution. He was murdered in Trawniki during Aktion Erntefest in 1943, and posthumously recognized as a spiritual guide for generations.

name: Rabbi Pinchas Horowitz (Ba'al Hafla'ah)
bio: Rabbi Pinchas Horowitz (1730–1805), known as the 'Ba'al Hafla’ah,' was the Rabbi of Frankfurt and a leading halakhist, disciple of the Maggid of Mezeritch, and a bridge between Hasidic and Misnagdic worlds.

name: אהרן קוטלר (Aharon Kotler)
bio: Rabbi Aharon Kotler (1891–1962) was a leading Lithuanian rosh yeshiva and founder of Beth Medrash Govoha in Lakewood, New Jersey. A disciple of the Slabodka yeshiva, he became rosh yeshiva of Kletsk in Poland before escaping to the United States during the Holocaust. He was a central leader in transplanting the Lithuanian yeshiva tradition to America and a founder of Chinuch Atzmai, the network of Torah schools in Israel.

name: Rabbi Aaron Uri Phoebus Hart
bio: Rabbi Aaron Uri Phoebus Hart (1670–1756) was a British rabbi, widely regarded as the first chief rabbi of Great Britain. Born in Breslau, Silesia, to Hartwig Moses Hart, he studied in Poland and married the daughter of R. Samuel ben Phoebus of Fürth. In 1704/5 he was appointed rabbi of the first Ashkenazic synagogue in London, the Great Synagogue at Duke’s Place, where he served until his death. His tenure was marked by communal disputes, most notably the controversy surrounding Marcus Moses and the Hambro synagogue. In 1707 he published 'Urim ve-Tummim,' the first Hebrew book printed entirely in London. A portrait of Rabbi Hart is preserved in the National Portrait Gallery, London.

name: Rabbi Aharon Perlov of Karlin (the Great)
bio: Rabbi Aharon Perlov of Karlin (1736–1772), known as Rabbi Aharon the Great, was one of the earliest Rebbes of the Hasidic movement and a disciple of Rabbi Dov Ber of Mezeritch, the Maggid. He founded the Karlin Hasidic dynasty in Karlin, near Pinsk, and together with Rabbi Menachem Mendel of Vitebsk spread Hasidism throughout Lithuania. Opponents of the Hasidic movement often referred to all Hasidim as 'Karlins' due to his influence. He was known for introducing fervent, emotional prayer marked by great enthusiasm and shouting, a hallmark of Karlin Hasidism to this day. He authored the famous piyyut 'Ya Aksuf', sung in many Hasidic courts on Shabbat. He passed away at the young age of 36, leaving behind a legacy of passionate devotion and outreach. His disciple Rabbi Shlomo of Karlin succeeded him, raising his son Rabbi Asher of Stolin, continuing the Karlin-Stolin dynasty.

name: Rabbi Yehuda Amital
bio: Orthodox rabbi, Holocaust survivor, founder and Rosh Yeshiva of Yeshivat Har Etzion for 40 years, creator of the Hesder Yeshiva model combining Torah study with IDF service, and political leader associated with the Israeli Left. He also served briefly as a minister in the Israeli government after the assassination of Prime Minister Yitzhak Rabin.

name: ר׳ אהרן יהודה לייב שטיינמן (Aharon Yehuda Leib Shteinman)
bio: Rabbi Aharon Yehuda Leib Shteinman (1914–2017) was a leading Lithuanian Torah sage, Rosh Yeshiva, and spiritual guide of the Haredi world. Known for his humility, simplicity, and clarity, he served as Rosh Yeshiva of Gaon Yaakov in Bnei Brak and was widely regarded as the successor to Rav Yosef Shalom Elyashiv as leader of the Torah world.

name: אריה קפלן (Aryeh Kaplan)
bio: Rabbi Aryeh Kaplan (1934–1983) was an American Orthodox rabbi, physicist, and prolific author, known for making complex Jewish mystical and philosophical concepts accessible. With a background in physics and deep knowledge of Kabbalah, he wrote over 50 works during his short lifetime, including translations, commentaries, and original books. His writings continue to inspire ba'alei teshuva, scholars, and seekers worldwide.

name: ר׳ אשר אריאלי (Asher Arieli)
bio: Rabbi Asher Arieli (b. 1957) is the senior maggid shiur of the Mir Yeshiva in Jerusalem. His daily shiur, attended by over a thousand students, is considered one of the largest regular Torah lectures in the world. He is renowned for his clarity, depth, and ability to connect intricate Talmudic sugyot across the Bavli and Rishonim.

name: אשר וייס (Asher Weiss)
bio: Rabbi Asher Weiss (b. 1953) is a leading halakhic authority, Rosh Kollel of Machon Minchas Asher in Jerusalem, and posek for Shaarei Tzedek Medical Center. He is known for his vast Torah knowledge, clarity in psak halakha, and ability to address complex issues of modern life and medicine. Author of the acclaimed 'Minchas Asher' series, he is sought worldwide for halakhic rulings.

name: Rabbi Baruch HaLevi Epstein
bio: Rabbi Baruch HaLevi Epstein (1860–1942) was a Lithuanian rabbi and scholar, son of Rabbi Yechiel Michel Epstein, author of the Aruch HaShulchan, and Michla, sister of the Netziv of Volozhin. He is best known as the author of Torah Temimah, a classic commentary that connects the Written Torah with the Oral Torah. He was educated at the Volozhin Yeshiva, ordained by leading rabbis including his uncle the Netziv and Rabbi Yosef Dov Soloveitchik, and later studied in Vienna. Though he worked for many years as a banker in Pinsk, he continued intensive Torah study and writing. He was active in the Zion Association for Hebrew language revival, and his correspondence during the Beilis blood libel trial defending the Jewish people was widely publicized. He traveled to the United States between 1923 and 1926 to publish his works. He refused rabbinical positions in Pinsk, St. Petersburg, and Moscow, preferring to focus on scholarship. He died in the Holocaust in 1942 in Pinsk and was buried in Karlin.

name: Sir Israel Brodie
bio: Chief Rabbi of Great Britain and the Commonwealth (1948–1965). A dignified leader, mellifluous preacher, and organiser of the Conference of European Rabbis, he helped rebuild European Jewry after the Holocaust and oversaw the post-war expansion of Anglo-Jewry. He was the first Chief Rabbi to be knighted.

name: Rabbi Chaim ben Attar (the Elder)
bio: Rabbi Chaim ben Attar (d. 1720), known as 'the Elder,' was the head of the great yeshiva in Salé, Morocco, housed in what became known as the Ben Attar Synagogue. He was the grandfather and teacher of the famed kabbalist Rabbi Chaim ibn Attar (the Or HaChaim). He was renowned for his diligence, rising nightly to recite Tikkun Chatzot with tears, and for hosting Torah scholars in his home, which served as a gathering place for rabbis. He studied under Rabbi Chiya Dayan and Rabbi Elisha Chaim Ashkenazi, and later became a central rabbinic authority in Salé. Following the death of his brother and business partner Shem Tov ben Attar in 1705, he traveled to Meknes with his son Moshe and grandson Chaim to oversee family business, but persecution forced the family to flee. He remained in Meknes for two and a half years with the Nagid Rabbi Moshe De Avila. Among his prominent students were his grandson Rabbi Chaim ibn Attar, Shmuel de Avila, and Rabbi Shalom Buzaglo. He lived to an advanced age, reportedly over 100, and passed away in 1720. Rabbi Moshe Berdugo delivered a sermon in his memory.

name: R' Chaim HaLevi Soloveitchik (Rav Chaim of Brisk)
bio: Rav Chaim HaLevi Soloveitchik (1853–1918), known as Rav Chaim of Brisk, was one of the greatest rabbinic figures of the 20th century and the father of the Brisker method of Talmudic study. Born in Volozhin to Rabbi Yosef Dov (Beis HaLevi) Soloveitchik and Zirl née Efron, he studied in the Volozhin Yeshiva and later married Lipsha, daughter of Rabbi Raphael Shapira, son-in-law of the Netziv. He became deputy head of the Volozhin Yeshiva at age 27 and later the rabbi of Brisk. He was renowned for his innovative analytical method of learning, his stringency in matters of pikuach nefesh, his fierce opposition to Zionism and the Mussar movement, and his devotion to the poor and needy. His teachings laid the foundation for the Lithuanian yeshiva world, and his legacy was carried forward by his son, Rav Yitzchak Ze'ev Soloveitchik (the Brisker Rav).

name: R'Chaim Kanievsky
bio: Rabbi Chaim Kanievsky (1928–2022) was widely revered as the Gadol HaDor, the preeminent Litvak Torah authority of his generation. Born in Pinsk to Rabbi Yaakov Yisrael Kanievsky (the Steipler) and Pesha Miriam (sister of the Chazon Ish), he grew up in Bnei Brak, where he lived his entire adult life. Renowned for his encyclopedic mastery of Torah, he completed Shas at age 17 and later authored dozens of seforim. Thousands flocked daily to his modest home for blessings and halachic guidance. After the passing of Rav Elyashiv and Rav Shteinman, he was regarded as the supreme halachic leader of the Haredi world. His funeral in 2022 drew over half a million people, among the largest in Israel’s history.

name: ר׳ אברהם חיים נאה (Avraham Chaim Naeh)
bio: Rav Avraham Chaim Naeh (1890–1954) was a leading posek in Jerusalem, renowned for establishing standard halachic measurements (shiurim). Born in Hebron, he studied in Jerusalem under the leading rabbis of the Old Yishuv and later became dayan and halachic authority of the Chabad community. His works Kezot HaShulchan and Shiurei Torah became foundational in clarifying halachic practice.

name: Chaim Ozer Grodzinski
bio: Rabbi Chaim Ozer Grodzinski (1863–1940) was Av Beis Din of Vilna, a leading posek and Torah scholar. He led the Va'ad ha-Yeshivot, guided Lithuanian Jewry for decades, co-founded Agudath Israel, and authored the responsa sefer Achiezer.

name: חיים שמולביץ (Chaim Shmulevitz)
bio: Rabbi Chaim Shmulevitz (1902–1979) was a renowned Torah scholar and Rosh Yeshiva of the Mir Yeshiva. Born in Kovno, he was raised in the Slabodka tradition of mussar and studied under Rav Eliezer Yehuda Finkel. He became a central figure in the Mir Yeshiva's wartime escape to Shanghai, where he sustained the yeshiva throughout the war. After immigrating to Eretz Yisrael, he became Rosh Yeshiva of Mir in Jerusalem, delivering legendary mussar discourses remembered for their passion and depth.

name: Rabbi Chaim of Volozhin
bio: Rabbi Chaim of Volozhin (1749–1821), also known as Chaim ben Yitzchok Ickovits, was a preeminent Lithuanian rabbi, Talmudist, ethicist, and disciple of the Vilna Gaon. Revered as 'Reb Chaim Volozhiner,' he was famed for his immense diligence in Torah study, his humility, and his mastery of both revealed and esoteric Torah. In 1803 he founded the Volozhin Yeshiva (Etz Chaim), which became the 'mother of all Lithuanian yeshivas' and set the model for modern yeshiva education across Eastern Europe. His primary work, Nefesh HaChaim, presented a systematic Lithuanian response to Hasidism and emphasized the primacy of Torah study, prayer, and service of God. He also authored Ruach Chaim, a commentary on Pirkei Avot. His influence shaped generations of Lithuanian Jewry, and among his descendants were the Netziv and the Soloveitchik dynasty.

name: ר׳ סיריל קליין האריס (Cyril K. Harris)
bio: Rabbi Cyril Kitchener Harris (1936–2005) served as Chief Rabbi of South Africa from 1987 to 2004. A British-born rabbi educated at Jews’ College, London, he became known for his moral leadership during the end of apartheid. He worked closely with Nelson Mandela and emphasized reconciliation, justice, and Jewish responsibility in society.

name: Rabbi David ben Hassin
bio: Rabbi David ben Hassin (1727–1792) was one of the greatest Jewish poets of Morocco, whose piyyutim spread across the Maghreb and the Middle East. Born in the Mellah of Meknes, he studied under Rabbi Mordechai Birdugo and married his daughter. At 17, he authored his first work, Migdal LaDavid, on biblical exegesis and Torah study. He became a prolific poet, composing over 250 poems blending biblical, rabbinic, and liturgical Hebrew. His works, filled with themes of exile, suffering, consolation, and redemption, gave voice to Moroccan Jewry during a time of drought, persecution, and the brutal reign of Moulay Yazid. His poetry, including piyyutim such as 'Asdar Toshbechta,' was sung throughout Moroccan communities and later adopted in Yemen and other Eastern Jewish traditions. After his death, many of his poems were compiled and published in Amsterdam in 1867 as 'Tehillah LeDavid' (Praise for David), ensuring his legacy as a central poet of Sephardic Jewry.

name: אליהו אליעזר דסלר (Eliyahu Eliezer Dessler)
bio: Rabbi Eliyahu Eliezer Dessler (1892–1953) was a rabbi, mussar teacher, and thinker, best known for his collected teachings published as 'Michtav Me’Eliyahu'. A student of the Kelm Talmud Torah, he became mashgiach in Gateshead (England) and later at the Ponovezh Yeshiva in Bnei Brak. His writings emphasize free will, spiritual growth, and the struggle between giving and taking.

name: ר׳ דב לנדו (Dov Landau)
bio: Rabbi Dov Landau (b. 1930) is the Rosh Yeshiva of Slabodka in Bnei Brak and one of the foremost leaders of the Lithuanian Haredi world. Known for his fiery mussar shmuessen and clarity in Torah learning, he has guided generations of talmidim.

name: ר׳ גרשון אדלשטיין (Gershon Edelstein)
bio: Rabbi Gershon Edelstein (1923–2023) was Rosh Yeshiva of the Ponevezh Yeshiva in Bnei Brak and a leading authority of the Lithuanian Torah world. Known for his gentleness, clarity, and humility, he guided thousands of talmidim and, in his later years, served as the acknowledged leader of the Haredi world.

name: Elchonon Bunim Wasserman
bio: Rabbi Elchonon Bunim Wasserman (1874–1941) was one of the foremost disciples of the Chofetz Chaim and a leading Torah educator of prewar Europe. Born in Biržai, Lithuania, he studied at Telshe, Brisk under Rav Chaim Soloveitchik, and later in Radin with the Chofetz Chaim. In 1921 he became Rosh Yeshiva of Ohel Torah-Baranovich, raising generations of talmidim with his clarity in learning and steadfast faith. He traveled to America to raise funds but returned to Poland before the war to remain with his students. In 1941 he was martyred in Kaunas together with other rabbis, urging his followers to accept their fate as korbanot for Klal Yisrael. His writings and students carried on his legacy, securing his place as a spiritual heir to the Chofetz Chaim.

name: Rabbi Eliezer Yehuda Finkel
bio: Rabbi Hezekiah Eliezer Yehuda Finkel (1879–1965) was the Rosh Yeshiva of the Mir Yeshiva in Poland and later its re-establishment in Eretz Yisrael. Born in Kelm to Gittel and Rabbi Nosson Tzvi Finkel (the Alter of Slabodka), he studied in Telz under Rabbi Eliezer Gordon and Rabbi Shimon Shkop, in Brisk under Rabbi Chaim Soloveitchik, and in many other yeshivot. In 1897 he was among the group of students sent by his father to strengthen the Slutsk Yeshiva under the Ridvaz. He later studied in Mir, where he married Malka, daughter of Rabbi Eliyahu Baruch Kamai, and eventually succeeded his father-in-law as Rosh Yeshiva. During World War I he led the yeshiva in exile to Ukraine, and after World War II reestablished the Mir in Jerusalem. He served as a member of the Council of Torah Sages of Agudat Yisrael and was renowned for his scholarship, leadership, and piety. After losing family in the Holocaust, he abstained from eating meat for the last 25 years of his life. He passed away in 1965 and was buried on Har HaMenuchot in Jerusalem.

name: Eliyahu Lopian
bio: Leading 20th-century Mussar rabbi, rosh yeshiva and mashgiach in London and later Israel.

name: Rabbi Yosef Shalom Elyashiv
bio: Rabbi Yosef Shalom Elyashiv (1910–2012) was one of the foremost poskim (halakhic authorities) of the 20th–21st centuries. Born in Šiauliai, Lithuania, he moved as a child to Jerusalem, where he became a leading disciple of the Chazon Ish and developed into the undisputed halakhic leader of the Lithuanian Torah world. He served briefly as a dayan in the Rabbinical Courts before devoting himself entirely to Torah study and psak halakha. His small study hall in Mea Shearim became the center of global halakhic guidance until his passing at age 102.

name: Rabbi Joseph ben Emanuel Ergas
bio: Rabbi Joseph ben Emanuel Ergas (1685–1730) was an Italian rabbi and kabbalist, renowned for his erudition and as a fierce critic of Sabbateanism. Born in Livorno to Rabbi Emanuel Ergas, head of the community, he studied Torah under Rabbi Samuel De Pas and briefly Kabbalah under Rabbi Benjamin Kohen Vitale, a disciple of Moses Zacuto. After marrying in 1704, he founded the 'Noah Shalom' yeshiva in Pisa, and established charities such as Mohar Batulot and Malbish Aryamin. Returning to Livorno, he became head of the community and a leading halakhic authority, answering questions from rabbis throughout Italy and France. His most famous disciple was Rabbi Malachi ben Jacob ha-Kohen, author of Yad Malachi. Ergas became especially noted for his polemics against the Sabbatean Neḥemiah Ḥayyun, whom he expelled from Livorno in 1710, and against Sabbatean ideology in general. He authored works including the anti-Sabbatean tracts Tokhaḥat Meguleh and Ha-Tzad Naḥash, and the kabbalistic classic Shomer Emunim, which defended the non-literal interpretation of tzimtzum. He died in Livorno in 1730 at age 45.

name: ישראל יעקב פישר (Yisrael Yaakov Fisher)
bio: Rabbi Yisrael Yaakov Fisher (1928–2003) was a leading halachic authority in Jerusalem and one of the senior dayanim of the Edah HaChareidis. He was known for his deep halachic rulings, humility, and leadership within the ultra-Orthodox community of Jerusalem.

name: Joseph Herman Hertz
bio: Chief Rabbi of the British Empire (1913–1946). A biblical scholar, eloquent preacher, and vigorous defender of Orthodoxy and Zionism. Guided Anglo-Jewry through both World Wars and the Holocaust. Known for his Torah commentary and his global 'Imperial tour' of Jewish communities.

name: יצחק הלוי הרצוג (Yitzchak HaLevi Herzog)
bio: Rabbi Yitzchak HaLevi Herzog (1888–1959) was the first Chief Rabbi of the State of Israel and previously Chief Rabbi of Ireland. Known as the 'Sinai of his generation' for his vast Torah knowledge, he worked tirelessly to integrate Jewish law into the emerging State of Israel, while also striving to rescue European Jewry during and after the Holocaust. He was the father of Israeli President Chaim Herzog and grandfather of current President Isaac Herzog.

name: שמשון רפאל הירש (Samson Raphael Hirsch)
bio: German Orthodox rabbi (1808–1888). Intellectual founder of the Torah im Derech Eretz approach, which emphasized combining Torah observance with worldly involvement. Served as rabbi in Oldenburg, Emden, and Nikolsburg, and from 1851 led the secessionist Orthodox community in Frankfurt am Main. Vocal opponent of Reform and proto-Zionism. Author of Nineteen Letters, Horeb, and extensive Torah commentary.

name: יצחק הוטנר (Yitzchak Hutner)
bio: Rabbi Yitzchak Hutner (1906–1980) was a major 20th-century Torah thinker, rosh yeshiva of Yeshiva Rabbi Chaim Berlin in Brooklyn, and author of 'Pachad Yitzchak'. He combined Lithuanian Torah learning with deep philosophical and mussar insights, shaping generations of American Orthodox rabbinic leadership.

name: איסר זלמן מלצר (Isser Zalman Meltzer)
bio: Rabbi Isser Zalman Meltzer (1870–1953) was a leading Lithuanian Torah scholar and Rosh Yeshiva, author of the classic halachic work Even HaEzel. He studied in Volozhin and under Rav Chaim Soloveitchik, later serving as Rosh Yeshiva in Slutsk. After immigrating to Eretz Yisrael, he became head of the Etz Chaim Yeshiva in Jerusalem. Teacher and mentor to many future Torah leaders, including his son-in-law Rav Aharon Kotler.

name: Rabbi Aharon Lichtenstein
bio: Orthodox rabbi, rosh yeshiva, and Israel Prize laureate. Born in Paris, raised in the U.S., he studied under Rabbi Joseph B. Soloveitchik (his future father-in-law) and earned a PhD in English Literature from Harvard. He served as Rosh Kollel at Yeshiva University before making aliyah to join Rabbi Yehuda Amital at Yeshivat Har Etzion in 1971. Known for integrating rigorous Torah study with a centrist Orthodox embrace of modernity, he became one of the most influential Torah leaders of his generation.

name: אליעזר מלמד (Eliezer Melamed)
bio: Rabbi Eliezer Melamed (b. 1961) is an Israeli Religious Zionist rabbi, Rosh Yeshiva of Har Bracha, and author of the widely popular halakhic series Peninei Halakha. A student of Rabbi Zvi Yehuda Kook, he is a prominent figure in Religious Zionist Torah leadership, known for making halakha accessible to the broader public. He is also a columnist and teacher, addressing both halakhic and ideological issues.

name: משה קורדובירו (Ramak)
bio: Rabbi Moshe ben Yaakov Cordovero (1522–1570), known as the Ramak, was a preeminent Kabbalist in Safed before the Arizal. He synthesized and systematized earlier Kabbalistic teachings into a unified philosophy. His works profoundly influenced later Kabbalah, Hasidism, and Jewish thought.

name: ר׳ משה הלל הירש (Moshe Hillel Hirsch)
bio: Rabbi Moshe Hillel Hirsch (b. 1932) is the Rosh Yeshiva of Slabodka Yeshiva in Bnei Brak and a member of the Moetzes Gedolei HaTorah. A talmid of the Chevron Yeshiva and close disciple of Rav Aharon Kotler, he is known for his penetrating shiurim and guidance to talmidim.

name: R'Moshe Rosen
bio: R'Moshe Rosen.

name: Rabbi Moshe Shapira
bio: Rabbi Moshe Shapira (1946–2017) was a prominent Israeli Torah thinker, lecturer, and guide for thousands of students across the yeshiva world and ba'alei teshuvah movements. He was a disciple of Rav Yitzchak Hutner, Rav Dov Schwartzman, and the Brisker tradition. He was renowned for his deep shiurim that combined halakhic analysis, aggadah, Midrash, and kabbalistic insight, and for shaping an entire generation of educators and thinkers.

name: Rabbi Moshe Soloveichik
bio: Rabbi Moshe Soloveichik (1879–1941) was an Orthodox rabbi and Rosh Yeshiva at the Rabbi Isaac Elchanan Theological Seminary (RIETS) of Yeshiva University. Born in Volozhin as the middle son of Rabbi Chaim Soloveitchik and grandson of the Beis HaLevi, he married Pesya Feinstein, daughter of Rabbi Eliyahu Feinstein of Pruzany. He served as rabbi of Raseiniai and later Khislavichi, and was director of Talmud studies at Tachkemoni Seminary in Warsaw. In 1929 he immigrated to New York, joining the faculty of RIETS as Rosh Yeshiva. He was the father of Rabbi Joseph B. Soloveitchik, Rabbi Ahron Soloveichik, and Professor Samuel Soloveichik, and grandfather to a generation of Soloveitchik scholars and leaders.

name: Rabbi Moshe Feinstein
bio: Rabbi Moshe Feinstein (1895–1986) was one of the foremost halakhic authorities of the 20th century. Born in Uzda, Belarus, he studied under leading Lithuanian rabbis and served as rabbi in Lyuban before immigrating to the United States in 1937. As Rosh Yeshiva of Mesivtha Tifereth Jerusalem in New York City, he became widely recognized as the preeminent posek (decisor of Jewish law) for Orthodox Jewry. His multi-volume responsa, 'Igrot Moshe,' address a vast range of contemporary halakhic issues, from medical ethics and technology to Jewish communal life. Revered for his clarity, compassion, and authority, Rav Moshe’s rulings shaped Jewish practice across the world.

name: Rabbi Nachman of Horodenka
bio: Rabbi Nachman of Horodenka (c. 1680 – 1765) was among the earliest figures of the Hasidic movement and one of the closest disciples of the Baal Shem Tov, who referred to him as 'the trustee of his house.' He was known for his righteousness, his dedication to the redemption of captives, and his ascetic way of life even before Hasidism was founded. Through his descendants he became the grandfather of Rabbi Nachman of Breslov. Rabbi Nachman immigrated to the Land of Israel and settled in Tiberias, where his presence greatly impressed contemporaries. He passed away in Tiberias on 2 Tammuz 5525 (1765) and was buried in the Baal Shem Tov Students' Plot in the ancient cemetery of Tiberias.

name: Nathan Marcus HaKohen Adler
bio: Chief Rabbi of the British Empire (1845–1890). First university-educated Chief Rabbi, known for unifying Anglo-Jewry and founding the United Synagogue.

name: אביגדור נבנצאל (Avigdor Nebenzahl)
bio: Rabbi Avigdor Nebenzahl (b. 1935) is an Israeli posek and Torah scholar, serving as the Chief Rabbi of the Old City of Jerusalem. A close disciple of Rav Shlomo Zalman Auerbach, he is known for his halachic rulings, clarity in teaching, and humility. He has authored numerous sefarim on Tanach and halacha and is considered one of the foremost living halachic authorities in Israel.

name: נתן צבי פינקל (Nosson Tzvi Finkel)
bio: Rabbi Nosson Tzvi Finkel (1943–2011) was the Rosh Yeshiva of the Mir Yeshiva in Jerusalem, one of the largest yeshivot in the world. Born in Chicago, he was a descendant of the Alter of Slabodka and came to Israel as a teenager. Despite suffering from Parkinson’s disease for decades, he built Mir into a massive Torah center with thousands of students. Known for his fiery mussar talks, personal warmth, and devotion to Torah.

name: Rabbi Raphael Berdugo (Archangel Raphael)
bio: Rabbi Raphael Berdugo (1747–1821), known as the 'Archangel Raphael,' was one of Morocco’s greatest rabbis, halachic authorities, and communal leaders. Born into the distinguished Berdugo rabbinic dynasty of Meknes, he was the grandson of Rabbi Moshe Berdugo ('Hamashbir') and son of Rabbi Mordechai Berdugo ('Marbitz'). Orphaned at fifteen, his brilliance in Torah was already recognized at his father’s memorial. Despite early poverty, he rose to become head of the Beit Din of Meknes and a leading posek of his generation. He enacted community regulations limiting ostentation, provided leadership during pogroms, droughts, and epidemics, and earned the reverent title 'Archangel Raphael.' His responsa and commentaries became foundational texts for Moroccan Jewry. He died on Hoshana Rabbah in 1821, at age 75, and his rulings continued to be revered long after his passing.

name: Rabbi Raphael Hayyim Isaac Carregal
bio: Rabbi Raphael Hayyim Isaac Carregal (1733–1777) was an itinerant rabbi and preacher from Hebron, regarded as the first rabbi known to have visited the American colonies. Ordained at 17, he spent his life traveling widely, serving in Constantinople, Curaçao, London, Jamaica, North America, Suriname, and Barbados. In 1773, while in Newport, Rhode Island, he befriended Ezra Stiles, future president of Yale, with whom he studied Hebrew, discussed biblical exegesis, and corresponded extensively in Hebrew. Carregal’s sermons, published in Newport in 1773, are the first known Jewish sermons printed in the United States. He died in Barbados in 1777 at the age of 43.

name: Yisrael Salanter
bio: Rabbi Yisrael ben Ze'ev Wolf Lipkin (1809–1883), known as Yisrael Salanter, was the founder of the Musar Movement, a leading Rosh Yeshiva, and a Talmudist. He emphasized ethical refinement and self-discipline as central to Jewish life, inspiring generations through his teachings and disciples.

name: Rabbi Sar Shalom Mizraḥi Sharabi (Rashash)
bio: Rabbi Sar Shalom Mizraḥi Sharabi (1720–1777), known as the Rashash, was a Yemenite rabbi, halachist, chazzan, and kabbalist. Born in Sharab, Yemen, he left for Jerusalem in fulfillment of a vow, passing through India, Baghdad, and Damascus. In Damascus, he engaged in halachic debate regarding the measure of a kezayit of matzah. In Jerusalem, he joined the circle of mekubalim at the Beit El Yeshiva, where his extraordinary piety and hidden mastery of Lurianic Kabbalah were gradually revealed. He rose to become Rosh Yeshiva of Beit El, leading the foremost kabbalistic academy of the time. He was deeply devoted to the teachings of Rabbi Isaac Luria and developed new methods of kavanot (mystical prayer meditations). Popular tradition recounts miracles associated with him, including resisting seduction in Yemen, an appearance of the prophet Elijah, and opening access for Jews to the Kotel. He was regarded by many as the gilgul (reincarnation) of the Arizal. His descendants included Chief Rabbi Chaim Abraham Gagin and later Shem Tob Gaguine. The Rashash passed away in Jerusalem in 1777 and was buried on the Mount of Olives. His yahrzeit is still commemorated annually by kabbalists and members of Beit El.

name: שלום שכנא מלובלין (Rabbi Shalom Shachna of Lublin)
bio: Rabbi Shalom Shachna (c. 1510–1558) was a leading Polish rabbi and head of the first great yeshivah in Lublin, making the city the center of Jewish learning in Poland. A disciple of Rabbi Jacob Pollak, he was one of the earliest exponents of pilpul. He was also the teacher and father-in-law of Rabbi Moses Isserles (the Rema).

name: Rabbi Hershel Schachter
bio: American Orthodox rabbi, posek, and rosh yeshiva at RIETS (Yeshiva University). A close student and assistant of Rabbi Joseph B. Soloveitchik, he became the youngest rosh yeshiva at RIETS in 1967. Widely regarded as one of the leading modern Orthodox halakhic authorities, he has written responsa on medical ethics, Jewish law during the COVID-19 pandemic, conversion, and communal leadership.

name: שלמה הלוי אלקבץ (Shlomo HaLevi Alkabetz)
bio: Rabbi Shlomo HaLevi Alkabetz (c.1505–1584) was a Kabbalist, poet, and rabbinic leader of Safed. He is best known as the author of the mystical hymn Lecha Dodi, sung worldwide to welcome Shabbat. Teacher of Rabbi Moshe Cordovero and close associate of the Safed circle of mystics, he helped spread Kabbalat Shabbat and Zoharic practices.

name: שלמה גורן (Shlomo Goren)
bio: Rabbi Shlomo Goren (1917–1994) was the first Chief Rabbi of the Israel Defense Forces and later Chief Rabbi of Israel. A brilliant talmid chacham and halachic authority, he became a symbol of religious Zionism and the integration of Torah with the modern Jewish state. He is remembered for blowing the shofar at the Western Wall during the Six-Day War (1967) and for his halachic rulings on issues of war, conversion, and national security.

name: ר׳ שלמה זלמן אויערבך (Shlomo Zalman Auerbach)
bio: Rabbi Shlomo Zalman Auerbach (1910–1995) was one of the foremost halachic authorities of the 20th century. Rosh Yeshiva of Kol Torah in Jerusalem, he was widely respected for his balanced rulings, kindness, and clarity in applying halacha to modern issues such as electricity, medicine, and technology.

name: משה שטרנבוך (Moshe Sternbuch)
bio: Rabbi Moshe Sternbuch (b. 1926) is a leading halakhic authority, dayan, and rosh av beis din of the Edah HaChareidis in Jerusalem. Born in London, he studied under Rav Elchonon Wasserman and became known for his brilliance from a young age. He later served as a rabbinic leader in South Africa before settling in Israel, where he became a major posek and dayan. He is the author of Teshuvos v’Hanhagos, a widely used multi-volume responsa work.

name: Joseph B. Soloveitchik
bio: Modern Orthodox rabbi, philosopher, and leader of American Torah Judaism.

name: Yitzchak Elchanan Spektor
bio: Rabbi Yitzchak Elchanan Spektor (1817–1896) was the Chief Rabbi of Kovno, one of the leading halachic authorities of the 19th century, renowned for his responsa, his leadership during crises, and his moderation toward modern movements while remaining firmly Orthodox.

name: Rabbi Isser Yehuda Unterman
bio: Third Ashkenazi Chief Rabbi of Israel (1964–1972), previously Chief Rabbi of Tel Aviv and Liverpool. A Mizrachi leader, renowned halachic authority, and disciple of Rabbi Shimon Shkop, he combined traditional Litvak methodology with attention to modern halachic issues.

name: ר׳ שלמה וולבה (Shlomo Wolbe)
bio: Rav Shlomo Wolbe (1914–2005) was a leading mussar thinker and educator in the 20th century. Born in Berlin, he studied at the Hildesheimer Rabbinical Seminary and later in Mir Yeshiva in Latvia. After surviving World War II in Sweden, he moved to Israel, where he became mashgiach of Be’er Yaakov and later founded Yeshivat Givat Shaul. He is renowned for his work Alei Shur and for shaping post-war mussar thought.

name: יעקב קמינצקי (Yaakov Kamenetsky)
bio: Rabbi Yaakov Kamenetsky (1891–1986) was a Lithuanian-American Torah scholar and Rosh Yeshiva of Torah Vodaath in Brooklyn. A student of Slabodka and a close colleague of Rav Aharon Kotler, he became a leading figure in American Orthodoxy. Known for his clarity, humility, and warmth, he authored Emes L’Yaakov on Torah and halacha, and played a central role in building Torah life in the United States.

name: יעקב פולאק (Rabbi Jacob Pollak)
bio: Rabbi Jacob Pollak (c. 1460–1541) was a pioneering Talmudist and rosh yeshivah, regarded as the founder of the Polish method of pilpul (analytical dialectical study). He was a central figure in establishing Poland as a major center of Jewish learning in the 16th century.

name: האדמו״ר מגור (Yaakov Aryeh Alter)
bio: Rabbi Yaakov Aryeh Alter (b. 1939) is the current Gerrer Rebbe, leading one of the largest Chassidic dynasties in the world. Known for his quiet but decisive leadership, he emphasizes Torah study, discipline, and community growth.

name: ירוחם ליבוביץ (Yerucham Levovitz)
bio: Rabbi Yerucham Levovitz (1875–1936) was the mashgiach ruchani of the Mir Yeshiva in Poland. A foremost disciple of the Alter of Kelm, he transmitted the Mussar tradition with depth, discipline, and clarity. His influence shaped generations of Mirrer talmidim, and his teachings are preserved in the multi-volume 'Daas Torah'.

name: Rabbi Yisrael Meir Lau
bio: Rabbi Yisrael Meir Lau (born 1937) is a Holocaust survivor, rosh yeshiva, and former Ashkenazi Chief Rabbi of Israel (1993–2003). Born in Piotrków Trybunalski, Poland, he survived the Buchenwald concentration camp as a child. After immigrating to Israel, he studied at yeshivot and became a prominent rabbi, serving as Chief Rabbi of Netanya, Tel Aviv, and later the State of Israel. He is noted for his eloquence, outreach, and efforts to preserve Holocaust memory. He is the father of Rabbi David Lau, current Ashkenazi Chief Rabbi of Israel.

name: האדמו״ר מבעלז (Yissachar Dov Rokeach)
bio: Rabbi Yissachar Dov Rokeach (b. 1948) is the Belzer Rebbe, who rebuilt the Belz Chassidic dynasty after the Holocaust. Known for his organizational skill and visionary leadership, he established schools, yeshivot, and a massive Beis Medrash in Jerusalem.

name: יוסף קארו (Rabbi Yosef Karo)
