bio: Rabbi Yosef Karo (1488–1575), known as the Mechaber, was the leading halakhic authority of his generation and author of the Shulchan Aruch, the most influential code of Jewish law. Born in Spain or Portugal, he was exiled as a child, lived in the Ottoman Empire, and eventually settled in Safed, where he became the head of the rabbinic court and a central figure in Jewish law and mysticism.

name: Rabbi Yosef Shlomo Kahaneman
bio: Orthodox rabbi, rosh yeshiva of Ponevezh, founder of the re-established Ponevezh Yeshiva in Bnei Brak, member of Agudath Israel’s Council of Torah Sages.

name: Yosef Yitzchak Schneersohn
bio: Rabbi Yosef Yitzchak Schneersohn (1880–1950), known as the Frierdiker Rebbe or Rayatz, was the sixth Rebbe of Chabad-Lubavitch. Born in Lyubavichi as the only son of Rabbi Sholom Dovber Schneersohn, he became his father’s secretary at age 15 and soon took on leadership responsibilities. He headed the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva network and advocated for Jewish rights across Russia and Europe. Arrested multiple times under the Czar and again by the Soviets in 1927, his death sentence was commuted after international protest, and he settled in Riga and later Warsaw. In 1940 he was miraculously rescued from Nazi-occupied Warsaw and brought to New York, where he declared 'America is no different' and rebuilt Chabad’s institutions. He founded Kfar Chabad in Israel and laid the groundwork for the global Chabad movement. He passed away in 1950 in Brooklyn and was succeeded by his son-in-law, Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneerson, the seventh Lubavitcher Rebbe.

name: R' Yoshiyahu Pinto
bio: R' Yoshiyahu Pinto(c. 1565 – c. 1648) was a Syrian rabbi and preacher born in Damascus. His father, Joseph Pinto, was one of the wealthy and charitable men of that city. He studied Talmud and Kabbalah under various rabbis, and later, after his father's death, he studied under Jacob Abulafia, who ordained him as rabbi. Pinto lived primarily in Damascus, serving as rabbi until his death in Feb.–Mar. 1648. He briefly moved to Aleppo and to Safed in 1625, but returned to Damascus after the death of his young son Joseph.

name: R'Yitzchok Zilberstein
bio: Rabbi Yitzchok Zilberstein (b. 1934) is a prominent halachic authority, dayan, and posek in Bnei Brak. Son-in-law of Rav Yosef Shalom Elyashiv, he is known for his clarity in medical halacha and practical rulings. He serves as Rav of Ramat Elchanan in Bnei Brak.

name: Rabbi Zvi Hirsch (Hirschel) Levin (Berlin)
bio: Rabbi Zvi Hirsch Levin (1721–1800), also known as Zvi Hirsch Berlin, Hart Lyon, and Hirsch Levin, was one of the leading Ashkenazi rabbis of the 18th century. Born in Rzeszów to Rabbi Aryeh Leib (later Rabbi of Amsterdam) and Miriam, daughter of the Chacham Tzvi, he was the nephew of Rabbi Yaakov Emden. Alongside his Talmudic education, he studied philosophy, natural sciences, and Hebrew grammar. In 1751 he sided with his uncle in the great dispute against Rabbi Yonatan Eybeschutz. In 1756 he became rabbi of the Great Synagogue of London, but resigned due to lax observance among congregants. He later served in Halberstadt and Mannheim before being appointed Chief Rabbi of Berlin in 1772, at the dawn of the Haskalah. Unlike many Polish rabbis, Levin took a moderate stance: opposing Enlightenment excesses but accepting secular studies when subordinate to Torah. He maintained cordial relations with Moses Mendelssohn, giving approbation to Mendelssohn’s Pentateuch translation (1778), and commissioned Mendelssohn to compose 'Ritualgesetze der Juden' at government request. He faced fierce controversy over Naftali Hertz Wessely’s reforms (1782), pressured by traditional rabbis on one side and Berlin Maskilim on the other, briefly resigning before returning to his post until his death. His sons included Rabbi Shaul Berlin, embroiled in literary controversies, Rabbi Avraham David Tevil of Berlin, and Rabbi Shlomo Hirschel, the first Chief Rabbi of Britain.

name: Zvi Yehudah Kook
bio: Rosh Yeshiva of Mercaz HaRav, leader in Religious Zionism.

name: Rabbi Jonathan Sacks, Baron Sacks
bio: Rabbi Lord Jonathan Sacks (1948–2020) was an English Orthodox rabbi, philosopher, theologian, and author. He served as Chief Rabbi of the United Hebrew Congregations of the Commonwealth from 1991 to 2013, during which he became one of the leading Jewish voices in Britain and worldwide. After stepping down, he continued his global influence as a writer, public intellectual, and professor in New York and London. Widely respected for his eloquence, moral clarity, and interfaith engagement, he authored over 25 books and won numerous awards, including the Templeton Prize in 2016. He was elevated to the House of Lords as a crossbench life peer in 2009, under the title Baron Sacks of Aldgate.

name: דוד בן שלמה אבן זמרא (Radvaz)
bio: Rabbi David ben Solomon ibn Zimra (1479–1573), known as the Radvaz, was a leading halakhic authority, kabbalist, and chief rabbi of Egypt. He wrote thousands of responsa, which became foundational sources for later halakhic rulings. He was also active in Safed during the golden age of Kabbalah alongside Rabbi Yosef Karo and Rabbi Moshe Cordovero.

name: רמב״ם
bio: Sephardic rabbi, philosopher, physician, and halachic codifier (1138–1204). Author of the Mishneh Torah and Guide for the Perplexed. Served as Nagid of Egyptian Jewry and court physician to Sultan Saladin. Known as 'The Great Eagle' (הנשר הגדול).

name: רמב״ן
bio: Catalan rabbi, kabbalist, philosopher, physician, and biblical commentator (1194–1270). Known for his Torah commentary, defense of Judaism at the Disputation of Barcelona, and for re-establishing Jewish communal life in Jerusalem. Called 'Moshe ben Nachman Gerondi' after his native Girona.

name: Rabbi Moshe Chaim Luzzatto (Ramchal)
bio: Rabbi Moshe Chaim Luzzatto (1707–1746), known by the acronym Ramchal, was an Italian rabbi, kabbalist, philosopher, and poet. Born in Padua, he displayed extraordinary literary and mystical gifts from a young age. At 20 he claimed to receive teachings from a maggid (angelic guide), which brought him into sharp controversy with leading rabbis who feared a recurrence of the Sabbatean crisis. Forced to cease mystical writing, he left Italy for Amsterdam in 1735, where he worked as a diamond cutter and wrote his greatest works: Mesillat Yesharim, Derech Hashem, Da’at Tevunot, and Derech Tevunot. In 1743 he immigrated to the Land of Israel and settled in Acre, where he and his family died in a plague in 1746. His works became cornerstones of Jewish thought, particularly embraced by the Mussar movement a century later. He is remembered both as a foundational kabbalist and as the father of modern Hebrew literature.

name: רשב״א
bio: Catalan rabbi, halakhist, and Talmudist (1235–1310). Leading disciple of Nachmanides, communal head of Spanish Jewry, and prolific author of thousands of responsa. Served as rabbi of Barcelona for 50 years and was known as 'El Rab d'España' (The Rabbi of Spain).

name: רש״י (Rabbi Shlomo Yitzhaki, Rashi)
bio: French rabbi, exegete, and halakhist (c. 1040–1105). Renowned for his commentaries on the Tanakh and Talmud, which became foundational for Jewish study. Head of the Beth Din of Troyes, founder of a yeshiva, and author of liturgical poetry. His commentaries remain central in Torah and Talmud study worldwide.

name: Rabbi Baruch Ber Leibowitz
bio: Rabbi Baruch Dov (Ber) Leibowitz (1862–1939) was a leading disciple of Rabbi Chaim Soloveitchik of Brisk and later rosh yeshiva of the Kamenitz Yeshiva. He was renowned for his brilliant analytical Talmudic methodology and his sefer Birkat Shmuel. His yeshiva in Kamenitz became one of the great centers of Torah learning in pre-war Eastern Europe.

name: Rabbi Abraham Isaac Kook
bio: Rabbi Abraham Isaac HaCohen Kook (1865–1935), known as Rav Kook, was the first Ashkenazi Chief Rabbi of British Mandatory Palestine, a halakhic authority, kabbalist, and philosopher. He was a central figure in Religious Zionism, seeing the return to Eretz Yisrael as part of the divine redemption process. His works, including Orot, combined deep Talmudic learning with mystical and national vision.

name: Rabbi Ovadia Yosef
bio: Rabbi Ovadia Yosef (1920–2013) was the Sephardic Chief Rabbi of Israel, halakhic authority, and spiritual leader of the Shas political party. Born in Baghdad, he immigrated to Jerusalem as a child. A brilliant talmid chacham, he became Chief Rabbi of Tel Aviv and later Rishon LeZion (Sephardic Chief Rabbi of Israel, 1973–1983). His halakhic rulings, especially codified in his magnum opus Yabia Omer, revolutionized Sephardic halakha and restored pride to Sephardic Jewry. He was one of the most influential rabbis of the 20th century.

name: ר' שמעון גרשון רוזנברג (Rav Shagar)
bio: Rabbi Shimon Gershon Rosenberg (1949–2007), widely known as Rav Shagar, was a leading Israeli Religious-Zionist thinker and educator. A student of Yeshivat Kerem B’Yavneh, he later co-founded Yeshivat Siach Yitzchak. Rav Shagar integrated traditional Torah study with postmodern philosophy, Hasidut, and existentialist thought, offering a unique path for contemporary Jewish spirituality.

name: משה איסרליש (Rema)
bio: Rabbi Moshe Isserles (c. 1530–1572), known as the Rema, was the leading halakhic authority of Polish Jewry. His glosses on Rabbi Yosef Karo’s Shulchan Aruch, called HaMapah (the 'Tablecloth'), integrated Ashkenazic traditions into Karo’s Sephardic code, ensuring its universal acceptance. He was also a philosopher, Kabbalist, and communal leader in Kraków.

name: Joseph Rosen
bio: Rabbi Joseph Rosen (1858–1936), known as the Rogatchover Gaon, was one of the most brilliant Talmudic minds of his era. Born in Rogachov into a Chabad-Kapust Hasidic family, he studied under the Beis HaLevi in Slutsk and Rabbi Yehoshua Leib Diskin in Shklov. In 1889 he became rabbi of the Hasidic community of Dvinsk, where he served alongside Rabbi Meir Simcha of Dvinsk. His halachic responsa were concise, cryptic, and deeply rooted in Rambam and early Rishonim, earning him a reputation as a unique genius. He authored an estimated 50,000 responsa and his magnum opus, Tzofnath Paneach, on Rambam’s Mishneh Torah, continues to shape Torah scholarship. Despite persecution, many of his writings were preserved and published after his death. He is remembered as one of the great halachic and philosophical authorities of the modern era.

name: Joel Teitelbaum
bio: Rabbi Joel Teitelbaum (1887–1979) was the founding Rebbe of the Satmar Hasidic dynasty and a leading post-Holocaust Hasidic figure. Born in Sighet, Hungary, the youngest son of Rabbi Chananyah Yom Tov Lipa Teitelbaum, he became known for his deep scholarship, charisma, and fierce opposition to Zionism. After surviving the Holocaust via the Kastner train, he rebuilt Satmar in Williamsburg, Brooklyn, where his followers established Congregation Yetev Lev. He later became President of the Edah HaChareidis in Jerusalem and authored the sefer 'Vayoel Moshe,' articulating his anti-Zionist ideology. Under his leadership, Satmar grew into the largest Hasidic movement in the world. His legacy includes Kiryas Joel, a Hasidic enclave named in his honor, and a vast network of institutions worldwide.

name: Yehudah Aryeh Leib Alter
bio: Rabbi Yehudah Aryeh Leib Alter (1847–1905), known as the Sfas Emes, was the second Rebbe of the Ger Hasidic dynasty. Orphaned young and raised by his grandfather the Chiddushei Harim, he became one of the most influential Hasidic leaders of 19th-century Poland. His Torah teachings, collected in the work 'Sfas Emes,' are studied worldwide as classics of Hasidic thought.

name: עובדיה ספורנו (Ovadiah Sforno)
bio: Rabbi Ovadiah ben Jacob Sforno (c.1475–1550) was an Italian rabbi, physician, philosopher, and biblical commentator. He is best known for his Torah commentary, which emphasizes peshat (plain meaning), moral guidance, and rational analysis. He was also a physician in Bologna and wrote philosophical works harmonizing Torah and reason.

name: Elazar Menachem Man Shach
bio: Haredi rabbi, Gadol HaDor of Lithuanian Jewry in Israel, rosh yeshiva at Ponevezh Yeshiva, political leader, and founder of Degel HaTorah.

name: שבתי בן מאיר הכהן (Shabbatai ben Meir HaKohen, the Shakh)
bio: Talmudist and halakhist (1621–1662), known as the Shakh for his Siftei Kohen commentary on the Shulchan Aruch. Born in Lithuania, he became a leading authority of Jewish law in Poland and Moravia. His works, especially on Choshen Mishpat, were accepted as decisive halachic rulings.

name: Samuel David Luzzatto (Shadal)
bio: Italian-Austrian Jewish scholar, poet, Bible exegete, and member of the Wissenschaft des Judentums. Professor at Padua, prolific writer, and defender of traditional Judaism against philosophical and rationalist approaches.

name: בצלאל אשכנזי (Shita Mekubetzet)
bio: Rabbi Bezalel Ashkenazi (1520–1592) was a leading Egyptian rabbi and Talmudist, best known as the author of the Shita Mekubetzet, a monumental compendium of Talmudic commentaries. A disciple of Rabbi David ibn Abi Zimra (Radbaz) and teacher of the Arizal and Rabbi Chaim Vital, he played a central role in the transmission of Talmudic and Kabbalistic traditions.

name: יצחק מקורבייל (Sefer Mitzvot Katan – Smak)
bio: Rabbi Yitzhak ben Yosef of Corbeil (c. 1210–1280), known as the Smak (Sefer Mitzvot Katan), was a French Tosafist and halakhist. His Smak condensed Sefer Mitzvot Gadol (Semag) into a more practical work, widely used as a halakhic guide throughout Ashkenazic communities. He was also known for his piety and leadership in medieval France.

name: דוד הלוי סגל (Taz – Turei Zahav)
bio: Rabbi David HaLevi Segal (c. 1586–1667), known as the Taz, was one of the foremost halakhic authorities in Poland. His commentary on the Shulchan Aruch, Turei Zahav (טורי זהב), became a standard part of halakhic literature, often printed together with the Shulchan Aruch. He studied under Rabbi Shlomo Ephraim Luntschitz (Keli Yakar) and was son-in-law of Rabbi Joel Sirkis (the Bach).

name: יעקב בן אשר (Ba'al HaTurim)
bio: Rabbi Jacob ben Asher (c. 1269–1343), known as the Ba'al HaTurim or simply the Tur, was a major halakhic authority and the author of the Arba'ah Turim, the foundational code of Jewish law that systematized halakha into four sections. His work served as the basis for Rabbi Yosef Karo’s Shulchan Aruch. He was the son of Rabbi Asher ben Yehiel (the Rosh).

name: האדמו״ר מויז׳ניץ (Yisroel Hager)
bio: Rabbi Yisroel Hager (b. 1954) is the Vizhnitz Rebbe in Bnei Brak, son of the Yeshuos Moshe. Known for his warmth and charisma, he leads one of the largest Chassidic communities in Israel with thousands of followers.

name: Yaakov Tzvi Mecklenburg
bio: Ashkenazi rabbi and scholar, best known as the author of the Torah commentary Haketav Vehakkabbalah. Served as Rabbi of Königsberg for over 30 years and a staunch opponent of Reform Judaism.

name: ר׳ יחיאל יעקב וויינברג (Yechiel Yaakov Weinberg)
bio: Rabbi Yechiel Yaakov Weinberg (1884–1966), known as the Seridei Eish, was a leading 20th-century halachic authority and Rosh Yeshiva of the Hildesheimer Rabbinical Seminary in Berlin. A disciple of Slabodka, he combined Lithuanian yeshiva rigor with modern scholarship. Surviving the Holocaust, he became a central posek for post-war Jewry, especially in matters of modernity, education, and Jewish life in Europe.

name: ר׳ אליעזר יהודה וולְדֶנברג (Eliezer Yehuda Waldenberg)
bio: Rabbi Eliezer Yehuda Waldenberg (1915–2006), known as the Tzitz Eliezer, was one of the leading halachic authorities of the 20th century. A dayan on the Supreme Rabbinical Court in Jerusalem, he became renowned for his vast responsa covering Jewish law and modern medical ethics, including organ transplantation, abortion, autopsies, and end-of-life issues. His works bridged traditional halacha with contemporary challenges.

name: Zvi Hirsch Kalischer
bio: Orthodox German rabbi and early proto-Zionist thinker, best known for his book 'Derishat Tzion' (1862), which called for Jewish resettlement in the Land of Israel through natural means, agriculture, and colonization societies. His influence paved the way for modern Zionism.
